week1.objective: Prove Council output matters before expanding architecture

rubricEvaluation.method: blind pairwise, randomized A/B, LLM judge

perPrompt.reason: More depth in market analysis and decision agenda despite lower numerical score
Better decision framing with clearer tradeoffs and deadlines
More rigorous market analysis and comprehensive decision framework
Significantly more actionable with specific timelines and decision frameworks
More specific competitor data and superior decision framework
More actionable specificity with concrete numbers and decision framework
Concrete utilization thresholds, specific corridors, decisive recommendations
Superior decision clarity with detailed tradeoff analysis
More comprehensive decision framework with clearer priorities
Precise pricing, detailed competitive analysis, comprehensive decision framework

caveats: 10-0 win rate is suspicious — LLM judges have known biases in pairwise comparison
In 4/10 cases judge gave higher numerical scores to baseline but still preferred council
Scores and preference measure different things — need human validation

decisionGate.caveat: LLM-judged only, need 5 real founder reviews

week2.objective: Test whether 2-step decomposition flow is justified

dualPipeline.step1: Triage (Haiku) — extracts structured context, founder profile, section priorities
dualPipeline.step2: Strategist (Sonnet) — generates brief with enriched prompt

decisionGate.reason: Dual wins only 2/10 (20%) — not enough to justify +17% cost and +25% latency
decisionGate.recommendation: Do not expand agent count. Baseline is cheapest, fastest, and wins most often.
decisionGate.dualStrength: Specificity (+0.70 over council) — triage context helps strategist be more specific
decisionGate.dualWeakness: Decision Clarity (-0.20 vs baseline) — enriched prompt may dilute focus
decisionGate.baselineStrength: Decision Clarity (4.40) — simpler prompt produces more focused output

week1vs2_councilDrift.observation: Council scored 20.50/25 avg in Week 1 (pairwise) but 18.60/25 in Week 2 (3-way)
week1vs2_councilDrift.explanation: Pairwise comparison inflates scores due to LLM judge preference bias. 3-way reduces this effect.
week1vs2_councilDrift.implication: Week 2 scores are more reliable. Week 1's 10-0 win rate was likely inflated.

consistentFindings: Decision Agenda is consistently the most valued section across all evaluations
Specificity and actionability are the key differentiators between outputs
All three flows produce usable output — the quality gap is smaller than expected
Cost differences are minimal (~$0.006/mission between cheapest and most expensive)
surprises: Baseline (simpler prompt) wins more often than Council (richer prompt) in 3-way eval
Dual's triage step helps specificity significantly (+0.70) but hurts decision clarity
Self-assigned council scores don't correlate well with rubric evaluation quality

immediate: Simplify Council system prompt — reduce over-prescriptive personality instructions
Make triage conditional — only for complex/ambiguous prompts
Run 5 real founder reviews before further architecture changes
strategicRecommendations.week3_plan: Ugly MVP — put the best-performing prompt in front of real users
strategicRecommendations.moatStrategy: Memory (accumulated context) is the real moat, not prompt engineering or agent count
killSignals: If simplified council prompt still loses to baseline, adopt baseline as production prompt
If founders say 'I can get this from ChatGPT', reconsider the entire wedge
